shower.thought: We assume we're looking for things in life, but we're probably just looking for the feeling we remember having when we first found them.
The reason we say "bless you" after a sneeze is the one superstition we've all collectively agreed to keep, and nobody questions it.
You've probably had the exact same thought as someone else at the exact same moment, and you'll never know it happened.
Money is the only thing we've invented that can simultaneously solve and create every major problem in your life.
We call it "falling in love" like it's accidental, but it's really the only time we're intentionally reckless.
The most stressful part of cooking for someone else isn't the cooking—it's the moment right before they take their first bite when you realize you can't control their opinion anymore.
Your phone knows more about your future than you do, and it's just waiting for you to check it.
We spend years learning to be independent, then spend the rest of our lives trying to find people to be dependent on again.
Congrats is short for congratulations, but nobody ever explains why we needed to shorten it when we were celebrating.
Every password you've ever forgotten was the most important one at the time.
You only know how much time you've wasted in your life while you're wasting more time realizing it.
The saddest part about growing up isn't losing your imagination—it's gaining the ability to see all the reasons why your ideas won't work.
We've invented machines to think for us, and now we're anxious that they think too well.
Someone out there has had the exact same embarrassing moment as you, probably at the same time, and you're both convinced nobody else would ever understand it.
The things you regret most are usually the moments you were too aware of yourself to just exist.
We call them "comfort foods" when really we mean "foods that taste like a memory from when things were simpler."
Sleep is the only thing that gets universally recommended when everything else fails, which is weird because it's the one thing we can't control anymore.
You'll never have a conversation with your future self, but every choice you make is you trying to convince them that you were right.
The fact that we have to schedule time to relax means we've failed at the whole relaxation thing.
Every story you tell about yourself is a lie, but not because you're dishonest—just because you're the worst witness to your own life.
Procrastination isn't laziness; it's your subconscious refusing to commit to a version of yourself that hasn't happened yet.
Your most honest moment with someone is usually when you're both too tired to pretend anymore.
The reason we ask "how are you?" instead of actually asking how you are is because we've collectively decided that some truths are too heavy for small talk.
Being "productive" has become the only socially acceptable form of self-harm.
We invented glasses to help us see better, but we're the only species that looks worse when we're trying our hardest to be ourselves.
The scariest part of getting older isn't that time speeds up—it's that you start recognizing the patterns and can't pretend you didn't see them coming.
You're probably going to have the exact same relationship with food as your parents did, and you won't realize it until you're complaining about it to your own kids.
The things we call "life experience" are just expensive mistakes we've convinced ourselves were worth it.
Your apartment is a shrine to decisions you made when you had more money and fewer questions.
We're all just trying to find the people who make us feel like the version of ourselves we actually like.
Silence in a conversation with someone you love is comfortable, and silence with a stranger is unbearable, which is basically the definition of the entire human condition.
The reason we take pictures of moments is because we don't trust our memories, but the pictures don't help—they just make us forget that we were actually there.
You've definitely offended someone without knowing it, and they've definitely forgiven you without telling you.
Every time you delete something you've written, you're mourning a version of yourself that nobody else ever gets to know.
The things you worry about at 2 AM are usually gone by morning, which means your anxiety has a bedtime.

motivational.thought: The obstacle you're avoiding today becomes the obstacle that avoids you tomorrow. — Unknown
Growth is what happens when you stop choosing comfort. — Unknown
You're not behind. You're just comparing your chapter 3 to someone else's chapter 20. — Unknown
The difference between who you are and who you want to be is what you're willing to do when nobody's watching. — Unknown
Small disciplines repeated with consistency over time create extraordinary results. — Unknown
Your excuses are rehearsed. Your effort should be too. — Unknown
You don't rise to the level of your goals. You fall to the level of your systems. — Unknown
The person you'll be in five years is being decided right now, in these small moments you think don't matter. — Unknown
Regret is just wisdom that arrived too late. Don't wait for it to show up. — Unknown
Progress whispers. Only failure screams. Learn to listen to the quiet. — Unknown
You're allowed to want something and still be grateful for what you have. These aren't opposing forces. — Unknown
The cost of discipline is always less than the cost of regret. — Unknown
You can't think your way to confidence. You have to act your way there. — Unknown
Comparison is the death of contentment and the birth of ambition. Choose which one you need today. — Unknown
What feels impossible now is only impossible because you haven't started yet. — Unknown
The version of you that's proud of yourself doesn't exist in the planning phase. — Unknown
You don't need permission to begin. You need a beginning to earn permission. — Unknown
Your standards are the only rules that actually apply to you. Make them count. — Unknown
Discomfort is the price of admission to the person you're trying to become. — Unknown
Everyone starts as a beginner. The difference is some people stayed there. — Unknown
The work you do when you don't feel like it builds the person who won't have to. — Unknown
Your future self is watching you decide right now. Make them proud. — Unknown
Discipline doesn't punish you. Lack of it does. — Unknown
You're not lost. You're just not where you're used to being yet. That's called progress. — Unknown
The gap between knowing and doing is where most people live. Don't be most people. — Unknown
Motivation is a feeling. Consistency is a choice. Pick the one that works when the feeling leaves. — Unknown
You don't need more time. You need to want it more than you want to scroll. — Unknown
The story you tell yourself about what you can do becomes the only boundary that matters. — Unknown
Frustration means you care enough to want different. That's the first step. Everything else is just follow-through. — Unknown
Your limitations are real. Your excuses are optional. — Unknown
The person who doesn't quit isn't special. They just decided that quitting wasn't an option. — Unknown
You're capable of so much more than your comfort zone allows you to prove. — Unknown
Success is just failure that didn't stop trying. — Unknown
The only feedback that matters is the one you give yourself when nobody's listening. Make it honest. Make it kind. Make it count. — Unknown

darkjoke.thought: I told my friend he was going to regret his tattoo. He said, "How could you possibly know that?" I said, "Because you're still alive."
My therapist asked why I never finish anything. I said that's ridiculous, I have a great track rec—
The problem with life insurance is it's only useful when you're dead. Which is also the problem with most of my relationships.
I'm not saying my family is toxic, but our group chat has more casualties than a season finale of Game of Thrones.
My doctor said I need to cut back on alcohol. I told him I'd consider it. He said, "When?" I said, "At the funeral."
People keep telling me to reach out if I'm struggling. I did once. They didn't pick up. Turns out they were just being polite.
I love how people say "you'll understand when you're older." Yeah, that's the plan. That's literally all I'm counting on.
My Fitbit tracks my steps, my heart rate, and my will to live. Surprisingly, only one of those is increasing.
I told my ex I was seeing someone new. She asked if I was happy. I said, "The therapist hasn't given a diagnosis yet, so I'm cautiously optimistic."
They say the best time to plant a tree was 20 years ago. The second best time is now. The worst time was when my dad said, "Hold my shovel," and then just left.

I asked my friend why he doesn't believe in therapy. He said, "Why pay someone to listen to my problems when I can tell them to a bartender for the price of drinks?" He had a good point. He's been going to the same bar for eight years. The bartender still doesn't know his name.
My suicide hotline volunteer job is really rewarding. Mostly because the turnover rate is so low.
I tried to make a joke about my crippling anxiety, but I was too nervous.
My wife asked if I'd die for her. I said, "No, but I'm open to it happening independently."
I love how divorce is called "breaking up." Like if you're not married, it's a breakup. If you are married, it's destroying your children's future for slightly different reasons.
The good news about being dead inside is you save so much money on self-care. The bad news is everything else costs way more.

I told my friend I was having an existential crisis. He said, "Have you tried yoga?" I said, "Yes, but standing on my head doesn't change the fact that I'm nobody." He said, "Fair point. Pilates, then."

My son asked me what depression feels like. I said, "Remember when you watched the whole season of your favorite show in one day?" He said, "Yeah, that was awesome." I said, "Exactly. Except it feels like that but forever and you hate the show."
I'm reading a self-help book about low self-esteem. It's helping. Turns out I'm not qualified to write one.
My gym membership is basically a monthly reminder that I hate myself. Which is weirdly motivating.
They say money can't buy happiness. But it can buy a gun. Have you ever seen a sad person with a gun? That's because they've solved their problems.
I told my therapist I think I'm becoming invisible. She didn't respond. Perfect. It's working.
My favorite part about hitting rock bottom is realizing you can't actually touch bottom. You just keep falling and people keep charging you for the descent.
I asked my friend if he ever felt like a failure. He said, "Only on Tuesdays." I said, "It's Monday." He said, "I know. I'm getting ahead of things."

My wife said she needs space. I said, "Fine. I'll book you a trip to the ISS. They take people up there, right?" She said, "That's not what I meant." I said, "I know. I just assumed if you wanted distance, you wanted distance."
I tried to write a sad story, but it kept getting too close to my autobiography. Now my therapist says I need to work on "boundaries." Apparently they apply to fiction too.
My doctor told me I have one year to live. I asked what I should do. He said, "Pay your bills upfront." Practical man. I respect that.
I love how people say, "Everything happens for a reason." Yeah, and the reason is usually "cause and effect." It's not comforting, but it's honest.
My friend asked if I believe in ghosts. I said, "I don't know. Do you believe in your childhood trauma?" He said, "Of course." I said, "Then yes. Ghosts are real. We all know ghosts."
